title: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections
authors: May S. ElSherif, Scott A. Halperin

items.subtopic: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding Pertussis Pathogenesis: Role of Virulence Factors
items.content: Pertussis toxin is the primary virulence factor in B. pertussis, influencing pathogenesis through ADP-ribosylation of G proteins, which affects lymphocyte circulation and serves as an adhesin for respiratory ciliated cells.
keyPoints: Pertussis toxin composed of a B oligomer-binding subunit and an enzymatically active A protomer.
ADP-ribosylates guanine nucleotide-binding regulatory protein (G protein) in target cells.
Contributes to mitogenic activity, lymphocyte circulation effects, and bacterial adhesion.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Differential Diagnosis: Pertussis vs. Parapertussis
items.content: While B. pertussis and B. parapertussis cause similar illnesses, B. parapertussis lacks the gene for pertussis toxin, making it typically milder.
keyPoints: B. pertussis is more virulent due to pertussis toxin production.
B. parapertussis does not express the gene coding for pertussis toxin.
Co-infections with B. parapertussis and B. pertussis have been documented.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Emergence of Pertactin-Negative Strains in Pertussis
items.content: The emergence of pertactin-negative B. pertussis strains is linked to immune pressure from vaccines, with these strains becoming dominant (>80% in some countries) since 2010.
keyPoints: Pertactin-negative strains have emerged due to vaccine-induced immune pressure.
>80% dominance of pertactin-negative strains in some regions since 2010.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Pertussis Attack Rates and Immunization Impact
items.content: Pertussis is highly communicable, with attack rates of 80–100% among unimmunized household contacts. In well-immunized populations, the rate drops to 20%. Despite widespread immunization, pertussis outbreaks occur every 3–5 years globally.
keyPoints: Attack rates: 80–100% in unimmunized households; 20% in well-immunized ones.
Cyclical outbreaks every 3–5 years despite vaccination efforts.
DTP3 coverage <50% in many developing nations, globally at 84% (2022).
WHO estimates: 40 million cases and 400,000 deaths annually, mostly infants <3 months.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Pertussis Epidemiology in High-Income vs. Developing Countries
items.content: In developing countries, pertussis remains a significant cause of infant morbidity and mortality despite decreased incidence due to improved vaccine coverage. In high-income countries, recent years have seen increasing pertussis outbreaks.
keyPoints: Developing countries bear 90% of the global burden of pertussis.
Historic low in US cases (1010) reported in 1976; subsequent increase noted.
US experienced outbreaks in 2005, 2010, 2012, 2014, and 2015.
Pertussis incidence increased among adolescents and adults starting late 1990s.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Age-Specific Pertussis Incidence and Impact on Infants
items.content: While pertussis is often considered a childhood disease, it affects all ages. In unimmunized populations, incidence peaks during preschool years. In highly immunized areas, infants <1 year are most affected.
keyPoints: Pertussis can affect all age groups; >50% of children contract it before adulthood in unvaccinated settings.
In North America, peak incidence is among infants <1 year who haven't completed the immunization series.
Infants <2 months old accounted for 84% of pertussis deaths in the US (2000-2017).
Annual mean cases, hospitalizations, and deaths in infants: 2957, 1122, and 15 respectively.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Attachment and Immunity in B. pertussis Infection
items.content: B. pertussis attaches to ciliated epithelial cells using adhesins like pertactin and filamentous hemagglutinin, binding to integrins possibly with pertussis toxin. Despite the emergence of pertactin-negative strains, no clinical differences are observed due to redundancy in adhesion mechanisms.
keyPoints: Attachment mediated by surface adhesins (e.g., pertactin and filamentous hemagglutinin) binding to integrin family proteins
Pertussis toxin may assist in attachment
No clinical differences with pertactin-negative strains due to redundancy of adhesins
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · PATHOGENESIS | IMMUNITY

items.title: Immunity and Vaccine Response in Pertussis
items.content: Both humoral and cell-mediated immunity play roles in pertussis. Immunity post-infection is not lifelong, with protection waning over time. Whole-cell vaccines induce a TH1/TH17 response, while acellular vaccines elicit a TH2-biased response.
keyPoints: Immunity after natural infection is not lifelong
Whole-cell vaccine immunity lasts 10–12 years; acellular vaccine immunity wanes within 2–4 years post-final dose
Natural infection and whole-cell vaccines induce a TH1/TH17-predominant response, while acellular vaccines stimulate a TH2-biased response
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · PATHOGENESIS | IMMUNITY

items.title: Understanding the Phases of Pertussis in Children
items.content: Pertussis progresses through distinct phases, starting with a catarrhal phase that mimics the common cold. This is followed by a paroxysmal phase characterized by severe coughing episodes, which can include posttussive vomiting and an audible whoop.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 7–10 days
Catarrhal phase symptoms: coryza, lacrimation, mild cough, low-grade fever, malaise
Paroxysmal phase duration: begins after 1-2 weeks
Cough frequency: several per hour to 5–10 per day
Complications occur during the paroxysmal stage
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Recognizing Atypical Pertussis in Adolescents and Adults
items.content: Adolescents and adults often present with atypical pertussis, characterized by a severe, prolonged cough. Specific signs include paroxysmal coughing, posttussive vomiting, and an audible whoop.
keyPoints: Atypical presentation in adolescents/adults
Cough characteristics: severe, prolonged, often paroxysmal
Specific signs: whoop and vomiting with cough
Additional features: night-time coughing, sweating episodes between coughs
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Complications of Pertussis: Recognizing High-Risk Groups and Common Complications
items.content: Pertussis complications are more prevalent in infants than older children or adults. Key complications include apnea, pneumonia, seizures, encephalopathy, and even death in young children. In adolescents and adults, complications like urinary incontinence, rib fractures, carotid artery aneurysm, and cough syncope may occur.
keyPoints: 27.1% of hospitalized infants <2 years with pertussis had apnea
9.4% had pneumonia
2.6% experienced seizures
0.4% developed encephalopathy
10 children (0.9%) died in a series of >1100 hospitalized infants
Pneumonia is reported in <5% of adolescents and adults, increasing after age 50
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosing Pertussis: Laboratory Confirmation and Specimen Collection
items.content: Clinical diagnosis can be challenging in older children and adults due to symptom overlap with other respiratory infections. Lymphocytosis is a common indicator among young children but not in adolescents or adults. Nasopharyngeal secretions culture remains the gold standard, while PCR has become more prevalent for its sensitivity and speed.
keyPoints: Lymphocytosis (absolute lymphocyte count >10^8-10^9/L) is common among young children
Culture of nasopharyngeal secretions is 100% specific
PCR offers increased sensitivity and quicker results than culture
Best specimen collected by nasopharyngeal aspiration
Cultures positive for a mean of 3 weeks post-onset, negative within 5 days after therapy
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Serologic Diagnosis in Pertussis: When to Consider and Its Limitations
items.content: Due to diagnostic challenges with culture or PCR in adolescents, adults, and those symptomatic for >4 weeks, serologic diagnosis is increasingly used. Enzyme immunoassays detect IgA and IgG antibodies; a two- or fourfold increase in antibody titer suggests pertussis. However, cross-reactivity among Bordetella species can complicate interpretation.
keyPoints: Serologic diagnosis gaining attention due to diagnostic challenges
Two- or fourfold increases in antibody titer suggest pertussis
Cross-reactivity of antigens like filamentous hemagglutinin and pertactin complicates seroconversion reliance
Criteria based on single serum specimen comparison with population values are being accepted
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Recognizing Pertussis in Different Age Groups
items.content: In children, the presence of paroxysmal cough, post-tussive vomiting, and whoop strongly suggests B. pertussis or B. parapertussis infection, with lymphocytosis further indicating B. pertussis. In adolescents and adults, pertussis should be considered if a cough persists beyond 14 days, is paroxysmal, follows vomiting, or occurs after exposure to confirmed cases, despite the absence of classic symptoms.
keyPoints: Paroxysmal cough, post-tussive vomiting, and whoop suggest B. pertussis or B. parapertussis in children.
Lymphocytosis increases likelihood of B. pertussis etiology.
In adolescents/adults, suspect pertussis if cough persists >14 days, is paroxysmal, follows vomiting, or occurs after exposure to confirmed cases.
Consider other infections like Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydia pneumoniae, adenovirus, influenza virus in prolonged coughing illnesses.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Pertussis | SUPPORTIVE CARE

items.title: Management and Supportive Care for Pertussis
items.content: Antibiotic therapy with macrolides is crucial early in the catarrhal phase to eradicate B. pertussis, though it does not alter the clinical course if initiated later. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole serves as an alternative for those allergic to macrolides. Supportive care includes hospitalization for young infants and severe cases, with a quiet environment recommended to reduce paroxysmal episodes.
keyPoints: Macrolide antibiotics are preferred for early pertussis treatment; trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is an alternative for macrolide allergies.
Antibiotics do not alter clinical course if given after the catarrhal phase.
Hospitalize young infants and severe cases due to high complication and death rates.
Quiet environment may help reduce paroxysmal episodes; $\beta$-adrenergic agonists, glucocorticoids, and cough suppressants are not proven effective.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Pertussis | SUPPORTIVE CARE

items.title: Isolation and Chemoprophylaxis for Pertussis Control
items.content: Hospitalized pertussis patients require respiratory isolation due to droplet transmission. Isolation continues 5 days post-macrolide therapy or until nasopharyngeal cultures are negative, which takes about 3 weeks without treatment. Household contacts should receive chemoprophylaxis within 21 days of cough onset in the index case.
keyPoints: Respiratory isolation for hospitalized pertussis patients is necessary due to droplet spread.
Isolation continues for 5 days post-macrolide therapy or 3 weeks without treatment until cultures are negative.
Chemoprophylaxis recommended within 21 days of cough onset in index case, especially for high-risk household contacts.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · INFECTION CONTROL MEASURES | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Immunization Strategies Against Pertussis
items.content: Active immunization is crucial for pertussis prevention. Whole-cell vaccines, though effective (85% average efficacy), have adverse effects and are still used in developing regions. Acellular vaccines, less reactogenic, are preferred in developed countries but require booster doses due to waning immunity.
keyPoints: Pertussis vaccine reduced cases by >90% post-1940 in North America.
Whole-cell vaccines have 85% average efficacy but can cause adverse events.
Acellular vaccines are preferred for their lower reactogenicity and require boosters due to waning immunity.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · INFECTION CONTROL MEASURES | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Pertussis Immunization Recommendations During Pregnancy
items.content: Immunizing pregnant women is recommended to protect infants under 2 months, with effectiveness rates of 90–93%. This strategy enhances passive antibody transfer and reduces infant pertussis incidence.
keyPoints: Pregnancy immunization prevents pertussis in infants <2 months with 90–93% efficacy.
Immunization during pregnancy is safe and increases maternal antibody transfer to the fetus.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · INFECTION CONTROL MEASURES | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: Which of the following species is known to cause a milder illness similar to pertussis in humans?
items.explanation: B. parapertussis causes an illness in humans that is similar to pertussis but typically milder.

items.explanation: Pertussis toxin ADP-ribosylates a guanine nucleotide-binding regulatory protein (G protein) in target cells.

items.explanation: B. pertussis is described as a slow-growing fastidious organism.

items.question: What has been attributed to the emergence of pertactin-negative strains worldwide?
items.explanation: The emergence of pertactin-negative strains is attributed to immune pressure resulting from the use of pertactin-containing acellular pertussis vaccines.

items.explanation: In highly immunized populations, the peak incidence is among infants <1 year of age.

items.question: Which adhesins mediate the attachment of B. pertussis to ciliated epithelial cells?
options: Adenylate cyclase-hemolysin toxin and pertussis toxin
Tracheal cytotoxic toxin and dermonecrotic toxin

items.question: What is the role of pertussis toxin in B. pertussis infection?
options: It causes local mucosal damage directly.
It induces paroxysmal cough by itself.
items.explanation: Pertussis toxin impairs host defense mechanisms, contributing to the pathogenesis of infection.

items.question: What type of immune response is predominantly elicited by natural infection and whole-cell pertussis vaccine?
items.explanation: Natural infection and whole-cell pertussis vaccine elicit a TH1/TH17-predominant immune response.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · PATHOGENESIS | IMMUNITY

items.question: Which phase of pertussis is characterized by coryza, lacrimation, mild cough, low-grade fever, and malaise?

items.question: During which phase of pertussis are complications most likely to occur?
items.explanation: Most complications occur during the paroxysmal stage when coughing episodes become more frequent and severe.

items.question: What symptom is uncommon in pertussis but suggests bacterial superinfection?
items.explanation: Fever is uncommon during pertussis and its presence may indicate a bacterial superinfection.

items.question: Which organism commonly causes secondary pneumonia in older children and adults with pertussis?
items.explanation: Secondary pneumonia in older children and adults is usually caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae or Haemophilus influenzae.

items.question: What is the primary purpose of antibiotic therapy for pertussis?
options: To alleviate symptoms during the paroxysmal phase
To eradicate the infecting bacteria from the nasopharynx
To prevent transmission to close contacts
To reduce the duration of cough in adults

Within 14 days of cough onset in the index case

After 3 weeks of cough onset in the index case

items.question: Which component is NOT typically included in widely marketed acellular pertussis vaccines?
items.explanation: Acellular pertussis vaccines do not contain whole B. pertussis organisms; they include components like pertussis toxoid and filamentous hemagglutinin.

items.subtopic: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY
items.assertion: The most important virulence factor of B. pertussis is pertussis toxin.
items.reason: Pertussis toxin ADP-ribosylates a G protein in target cells, affecting lymphocyte circulation and serving as an adhesin for bacterial binding to respiratory ciliated cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pertussis toxin is important.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.explanation: The assertion is false as B. bronchiseptica primarily infects domestic animals; the reason correctly describes its impact on animals.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.assertion: The introduction of an adolescent booster dose across North America was implemented by 2006.
items.reason: This was in response to the increase in pertussis incidence among adolescents and adults that began in the late 1990s.

items.assertion: Pertussis incidence peaks during the preschool years in unimmunized populations.
items.reason: In highly immunized populations like North America, the peak incidence is among infants <1 year of age.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: The duration of immunity after whole-cell pertussis vaccination is short-lived.
items.reason: Little protection remains after 10–12 years post-vaccination.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the duration of immunity is considered short-lived.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · PATHOGENESIS | IMMUNITY

items.assertion: The paroxysmal phase of pertussis is characterized by spasmodic coughing with posttussive vomiting.
items.reason: Paroxysms may be precipitated by noise, eating, or physical contact and are often worse at night.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the paroxysmal phase involves spasmodic coughing with posttussive vomiting, which can be triggered as described.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Serologic diagnosis is increasingly used for pertussis in adolescents and adults.
items.reason: Laboratory diagnosis of pertussis is difficult after >4 weeks of symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the difficulty with laboratory diagnosis explains the increased use of serologic methods.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Pneumonia in infants with pertussis is primarily caused by secondary infections.
items.reason: Primary B. pertussis pneumonia develops in older children, adolescents, and adults.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; primary B. pertussis pneumonia occurs in infants.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: The WHO recommends continuing the use of whole-cell pertussis vaccines for primary infant immunization series.
items.reason: There is evidence of early waning immunity among children who received acellular pertussis vaccine in infancy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why WHO recommends continuing whole-cell vaccines.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · INFECTION CONTROL MEASURES | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: Acellular pertussis vaccines have been shown to be less effective than whole-cell vaccines.
items.reason: Acellular vaccines are associated with fewer adverse events and are widely used in developed countries.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; acellular vaccines are effective, though they may require additional boosters due to waning immunity.
items.reference: Pertussis and Other Bordetella Infections · INFECTION CONTROL MEASURES | ☐ PREVENTION
